result.markdown:
--- title: "Functional connectivity" subtitle: "Seed-to-target correlation and seed-regressor GLM on the typed fmrimod substrate." jupyter: python3 execute: freeze: auto --- Functional connectivity is not a core fmrimod modeling seam, but the common seed-to-target operation should not force every user to rewrite Pearson correlation by hand. fmrimod gives you the typed substrate the analysis should sit on: a dataset that owns its timing, a **typed nuisance model** instead of a hand-built matrix, projection through the same `residualize` used by the first-level path, a compact seed-correlation primitive, and the same `fmri_lm` machinery when you want the seed signal as a GLM covariate. ## A Dataset, Not Bare Arrays Start from an `fmri_dataset`. It carries the data, the TR, and the sampling frame together, so every downstream step reads its timing from one typed source instead of re-deriving it. ::: {#6d2e93be .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np import pandas as pd import fmrimod as fm from fmrimod import baseline_model, design_matrix from fmrimod.residualize import residualize from fmrimod.stats import seed_target_correlation, seed_target_lm rng = np.random.default_rng(13) n_time, n_vox = 160, 9 # Voxel 0 is the seed; voxels 1-3 are genuinely coupled to it. data = rng.normal(size=(n_time, n_vox)) data[:, 1:4] += 0.45 * data[:, 0:1] motion = rng.normal(size=(n_time, 6)) # e.g. realignment parameters dataset = fm.fmri_dataset(data, tr=2.0) dataset.n_timepoints, dataset.n_voxels ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` (160, 9) ``` ::: ::: ## A Typed Nuisance Model The drift, intercept, and motion confounds are a **typed `baseline_model`**, not `np.column_stack([...])`. The model owns the column structure; you never hand-assemble the nuisance matrix. ::: {#2a615a2d .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} bmodel = baseline_model( basis="poly", degree=2, sframe=dataset.sampling_frame, intercept="runwise", nuisance_list=[motion], ) design_matrix(bmodel).shape # (time, drift + intercept + motion columns) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ``` (160, 9) ``` ::: ::: ## Residualize Through the Typed Path `residualize` dispatches on the typed model — pass `bmodel` itself, not its matrix. This is the same projection the first-level seam uses, so the cleaned signal is consistent with how fmrimod fits everything else. ::: {#3aec6eb4 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} clean = residualize(bmodel, dataset.get_all_data()) clean.shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ``` (160, 9) ``` ::: ::: ## Seed-to-Target Correlation For a cleaned ROI matrix, the effect-size estimate is one explicit call. The helper preserves the readable "seed versus target columns" shape and adds Fisher-*z* columns for group-ready effect sizes. ::: {#787dab5b .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} seed_target_correlation( clean, seed=0, target_names=[f"roi_{j + 1}" for j in range(clean.shape[1] - 1)], ).round(3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ```{=html}
target r n fisher_z z_variance
0 roi_1 0.387 160 0.408 0.006
1 roi_2 0.403 160 0.427 0.006
2 roi_3 0.379 160 0.399 0.006
3 roi_4 0.258 160 0.264 0.006
4 roi_5 0.092 160 0.093 0.006
5 roi_6 -0.124 160 -0.125 0.006
6 roi_7 0.095 160 0.095 0.006
7 roi_8 -0.008 160 -0.008 0.006
``` ::: ::: ## Seed as a GLM Covariate The fmrireg-style inferential version treats the seed time series as a sampled BOLD covariate. That path is a thin wrapper over `fmri_lm`: it fits each target on `[intercept, confounds, seed]` and reports the seed coefficient, standard error, *t*, and *p*. ::: {#b7d90017 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} seed_target_lm( dataset.get_all_data(), seed=0, confounds=design_matrix(bmodel), add_intercept=False, target_names=[f"roi_{j + 1}" for j in range(clean.shape[1] - 1)], ).to_frame().round(3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ```{=html}
target estimate se t p df
0 roi_1 0.416 0.081 5.141 0.000 150.0
1 roi_2 0.392 0.073 5.391 0.000 150.0
2 roi_3 0.330 0.066 5.019 0.000 150.0
3 roi_4 0.234 0.071 3.276 0.001 150.0
4 roi_5 0.077 0.068 1.137 0.257 150.0
5 roi_6 -0.122 0.079 -1.536 0.127 150.0
6 roi_7 0.081 0.069 1.166 0.245 150.0
7 roi_8 -0.007 0.076 -0.095 0.925 150.0
``` ::: ::: ## Back to fmrimod for Group-Ready Effect Sizes `seed_target_correlation` already includes the Fisher *z* and sampling variance `1 / (n - 3)`, which is exactly what the group seam (`fmri_meta`, `group_fit`) expects per connection. The lower-level `r_to_z` helper remains available when you already have correlations from another source. ::: {#c03c7a96 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} fc = seed_target_correlation(clean, seed=0) z = fc["fisher_z"].to_numpy() z_var = fc["z_variance"].to_numpy() pooled_r = fm.z_to_r(z.mean()) pd.DataFrame({ "fisher_z": z, "z_variance": z_var, }).round(3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ```{=html}
fisher_z z_variance
0 0.408 0.006
1 0.427 0.006
2 0.399 0.006
3 0.264 0.006
4 0.093 0.006
5 -0.125 0.006
6 0.095 0.006
7 -0.008 0.006
``` ::: ::: ::: {#263dabf3 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} round(float(z.mean()), 3), round(float(pooled_r), 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=7} ``` (0.194, 0.192) ``` ::: ::: The coupled targets (`roi_1`–`roi_3`) carry the visible correlation; the Fisher *z* and its variance are now in the shape the group-level tools consume. Motion correction, registration, and normalization remain outside fmrimod's scope — the substrate here begins at a preprocessed dataset. ## Next - [Group analysis](group-analysis.qmd) - [Datasets](datasets.qmd)
